scenarios.opening_query: Our building sold and the new owners' 'renovation notices' feel like a pressure campaign to empty us out.
scenarios.hidden_fact: I'm the ground-floor bakery, not an apartment — my commercial lease has five years left and no renovation-relocation clause, which is why they're squeezing.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I'm the ground-floor bakery — five years left on a commercial lease with no relocation clause. That's why they're squeezing.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We're apartment tenants — and the notices skip every relocation-assistance requirement the city imposes.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We're apartment tenants — and their notices skip the city's relocation-assistance rules.

scenarios.opening_query: My roommate left without warning and the landlord says I'm responsible for her half of everything.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Consumer Law > Problems Between Consumers
scenarios.hidden_fact: The lease part is settled — I re-signed solo; what's left is HER: half the back rent I covered plus the furniture she took, a private claim between us.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The lease is settled — I re-signed alone. What's left is her: the back rent I covered and the furniture she took.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the remaining dispute is with the departed roommate
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The landlord's the fight — he's demanding double rent under a 'joint and several' clause while re-listing the room himself.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The landlord's the fight — double rent demands while he re-lists the room himself.

scenarios.opening_query: There's an unauthorized person living in the unit I rent out and my actual tenant vanished.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Landlord Residential || Wills & Trusts > Probate
scenarios.hidden_fact: I learned my tenant died in the hospital in May — the occupant is her adult son, and her estate (rent, deposit, belongings, and his status) has to be dealt with through probate channels.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: My tenant passed away in May — the occupant is her adult son, and the rent, deposit, and belongings run through her estate now.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the tenant's death routes matters through the estate
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: My tenant's alive — she moved in with a boyfriend and sublet illegally to this stranger; a standard unauthorized-occupant eviction.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: She's alive — moved in with a boyfriend and illegally sublet. Standard unauthorized-occupant eviction.
scenarios.vetting_note: Tenant-death fact reroutes an eviction into estate handling.

scenarios.opening_query: The landlord cashed my rent checks for months and now claims I was never his tenant at all.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || Consumer Law > General
scenarios.hidden_fact: It turns out he doesn't own the building — he's a scammer who 'rented' the same vacant unit to three of us; fraud recovery, not tenancy law, is the case.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: He doesn't even own the building — he 'rented' the same vacant unit to three of us. It's fraud recovery.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a rental scam rather than a tenancy exists
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: He owns it fine — he's just rewriting history to dodge the repairs; my canceled checks make the tenancy.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: He owns it — he's just rewriting history to dodge repairs. My checks prove the tenancy.

scenarios.opening_query: My apartment complex towed every car without current stickers, including mine, during a holiday week.
scenarios.hidden_fact: The tow company skipped the 72-hour notice the statute requires and is charging 'storage' from day one — the tower's practice is the claim.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The tower skipped the required 72-hour notice and charges storage from day one — their practice is the claim.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether tow-statute violations are the operative claim
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: The tower followed the rules — management never sent the sticker-renewal notices the lease requires; the complex is the fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: The tower followed the rules — management never sent the renewal notices the lease requires.

scenarios.opening_query: Our senior building's new manager cancelled the shuttle, the social room, and now the grab bars 'for liability.'
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Real Property > Tenant (Residential) || General Litigation > Civil Rights || Real Property > ADA Issues/Animals/Accommodations
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's a federally subsidized senior property — stripping accessibility features targets disabled residents, and the fair-housing complaint route with HUD is the lever.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's federally subsidized senior housing — stripping accessibility targets disabled residents, and the HUD fair-housing route is the lever.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether fair-housing/discrimination law governs the cuts
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's an ordinary market building — the cuts break specific lease amenity promises, a contract-and-habitability fight.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's a market building — the cuts break specific lease promises.
scenarios.vetting_note: Subsidized-status fact opens the civil-rights channel.
